title: Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
short_title: Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill creates a commission of fifteen people to study slavery and how African Americans have been treated unfairly by the government from 1619 until today. The commission will gather evidence, hold meetings, and listen to people talk about these issues. After studying everything for eighteen months, they will write a report with ideas about how America can make up for the harm caused by slavery and discrimination, including possibly saying sorry officially and giving money to African Americans. Congress is giving twenty million dollars to pay for this work.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues
subjects: War crimes, genocide, crimes against humanity

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings and Purpose
sections.text: Congress finds these facts. About four million Africans and their children were enslaved in America and the colonies from sixteen nineteen to eighteen sixty five. The United States government legally supported slavery from seventeen eighty nine through eighteen sixty five. Slavery was wrong and cruel. It took away African people's lives, freedom, citizenship rights, and culture. It denied them the money they earned from their work. There is plenty of proof that slavery and discrimination still hurt African Americans today. After the Civil War, Reconstruction brought some progress for Black rights through the thirteenth, fourteenth, and fifteenth amendments to the Constitution, the Civil Rights Acts of eighteen sixty six and eighteen seventy five, and the Freedman's Bureau. But this progress was violently stopped and failed to keep African Americans safe. After slavery ended, the government at all levels continued to harm and disadvantage African Americans through laws and practices like sharecropping, convict leasing, Jim Crow laws, redlining (preventing Black people from buying homes in certain areas), unequal education, and unfair treatment by police and courts. This stole their work and prevented them from making important contributions to science, arts, business, and public service. The civil rights movement and other efforts to fix these problems were sabotaged on purpose and by accident. This made their achievements temporary and unable to last, which made racial inequality even worse. Examples of how the federal government discriminated against African Americans include creating the Federal Housing Administration with policies designed to keep neighborhoods segregated by race, making most African Americans unable to get Social Security benefits for the first twenty years, letting states control the GI Bill which allowed discrimination against African American veterans, and allowing labor unions to discriminate based on race through the Fair Labor Standards Act of nineteen thirty eight. Because of this historic and continuing discrimination, African Americans still suffer serious economic, educational, and health problems. This includes having nearly one million Black people in prison, an unemployment rate more than twice as high as White people, and having on average less than one sixteenth of the wealth that White families have. This gap has gotten worse over time, not better. The purpose of this law is to create a commission to study and develop reparation proposals for African Americans. This is because of slavery including the trade across the Atlantic Ocean and within America that existed from fifteen sixty five in colonial Florida and from sixteen nineteen through eighteen sixty five in other colonies that became the United States. Both federal and state governments supported slavery in their constitutions and laws. The commission will also study legal and unofficial discrimination against freed slaves and their children from the end of the Civil War to today, including economic, political, educational, and social discrimination. It will look at the lasting negative effects of slavery and discrimination on living African Americans and on American society. It will study how textbooks and digital teaching materials are being used to deny how inhumane slavery was and the crimes against humanity committed against people of African descent in the United States. It will examine how Northern states were involved in Southern slavery and how institutions like colleges, corporations, religious groups, and other organizations directly benefited from slavery. The commission will recommend ways to educate the American public about its findings to help with racial healing, understanding, and change. It will recommend appropriate solutions based on what it discovers and will submit its examination results and recommendations to Congress.

sections.title: Section 3  Establishment and Duties
sections.text: This section creates the Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans in the legislative branch of government. The Commission must do these jobs. First, identify, gather, and combine all relevant proof and documentation of slavery that existed in the United States and the colonies from sixteen nineteen through eighteen sixty five. The Commission's documentation and examination must include facts about capturing and getting Africans, transporting Africans to America and the colonies for slavery including how they were treated during transport, buying and selling Africans and their children as property in trade between and within states, how African slaves and their children were treated in the colonies and United States including taking away their freedom, using their work for profit, and destroying their culture, language, religion, and families, and the extensive denial of their humanity, sexual abuse, and treating people as property. Second, study and analyze how federal and state governments supported slavery in constitutional and legal provisions, including how much these governments prevented, opposed, or restricted efforts by formerly enslaved Africans and their children to return to their homeland. Third, study and analyze the effects of laws made by federal and state governments that were meant to discriminate or had discriminatory effects on formerly enslaved Africans and their children after they were finally recognized as United States citizens beginning in eighteen sixty eight. Fourth, study and analyze other forms of discrimination in public and private areas against freed African slaves and their children who were finally given their rightful status as United States citizens from eighteen sixty eight to today, including redlining, unequal education funding, and harmful financial practices. Fifth, study and analyze the lasting negative effects of slavery and the matters described in the purpose section on living African Americans and on American society. Sixth, recommend appropriate ways to educate the American public about the Commission's findings to help with racial healing, understanding, and change. Seventh, recommend appropriate solutions based on the Commission's findings. In making these recommendations, the Commission must address several questions including how the recommendations match international standards for fixing wrongs and injuries caused by the government that include full reparations and special measures as understood by various relevant international agreements, laws, and findings. The Commission must consider how the United States government will offer a formal apology on behalf of the American people for committing gross human rights violations and crimes against humanity against African slaves and their children. It must look at how federal laws and policies that continue to unfairly and negatively affect African Americans as a group and those that continue the lasting effects both materially and mentally can be eliminated. The Commission must study how the injuries from these matters can be reversed and provide appropriate policies, programs, projects, and recommendations for reversing the injuries. It must consider how any form of payment to the children of enslaved Africans should be calculated based on the Commission's findings. The Commission must decide what form of payment should be given, through what methods, and who should be eligible for such payment. It must consider what forms of satisfaction besides an apology should be put in place to help return dignity and racial healing and reconciliation. The Commission must also consider any other forms of rehabilitation or repayment to African descendants that are needed and what form and scope those measures should take. The Commission must submit a written report of its findings and recommendations to Congress no later than eighteen months after the date of the first meeting of the full Commission.

sections.title: Section 4  Membership
sections.text: The Commission will have fifteen members appointed as follows. Within sixty days after this law is passed, three members will be appointed by the President, three members will be appointed by the Speaker of the House of Representatives working with the House committee that handles this topic, and three members will be appointed by the President pro tempore of the Senate working with the Senate committee that handles this topic. Within sixty days after the Director is appointed, six members will be chosen by the Director and approved by a majority of the nine politically appointed members. These six people must be from major civil society and reparations organizations that have historically championed reparatory justice. All Commission members must be people who are especially qualified to serve because of their education, training, activism, or experience, particularly in African American studies and reparatory justice. No person who is a member of Congress or works for the federal government or any state or local government can serve as a Commission member. Members serve for the life of the Commission. If a member leaves, the vacancy will be filled the same way the original appointment was made. Seven members make up a quorum (the minimum number needed to conduct business), but fewer members can hold hearings. Any vacancy on the Commission will not affect its powers and will be filled the same way the original appointment was made. There will be a Chair and Vice Chair of the Commission selected together by the Senate majority leader and the Speaker of the House working with the committees that handle this topic. They serve for the life of the Commission. The Chair must call the first meeting of the full Commission within forty five days after all members are appointed.

sections.title: Section 5  Powers of the Commission
sections.text: To carry out this law, the Commission can hold hearings, meet at different times and places, take testimony, receive evidence, and have people swear oaths. It can also require people to attend and testify and to produce books, records, letters, notes, papers, and documents through subpoenas or other means. Subpoenas can be served by any person the Commission chooses. If someone refuses to obey a subpoena, the federal district court where that person lives, is served, or can be found, or where the subpoena is returnable, can issue an order requiring them to appear at any designated place to testify or produce evidence. If someone fails to obey the court's order, the court can punish them for contempt. Additional enforcement rules from sections one hundred two through one hundred four of the Revised Statutes apply if any witness fails to comply with any subpoena or to testify when summoned. A subpoena can only be issued by agreement of the Chair and Vice Chair or by a majority vote of the Commission when a majority is present. The Commission can make contracts with government entities, private entities, or people for goods or services, including research or surveys, preparing reports, and other activities needed to do its job, as long as Congress provides the money and follows applicable laws and regulations. The Commission can get information directly from any department, agency, bureau, board, commission, office, independent establishment, or part of the United States government related to any inquiry the Commission conducts, including confidential information which the Commission must keep secure. Each such entity must provide information directly to the Commission when asked. When the Commission requests it, the Administrator of General Services must provide administrative support services to the Commission on a reimbursable basis (meaning the Commission pays for them), and other federal departments and agencies can provide administrative support services as determined by their heads to be advisable and authorized by law. The Commission can accept, use, and dispose of gifts or donations of services or property. The Commission can use the United States mail system the same way as other government departments and agencies. Any subcommittee, member, or agent of the Commission can take any action the Commission is authorized to take if the Commission authorizes them to do so.

sections.title: Section 6  Administrative Provisions
sections.text: The Commission will have a Director who will be chosen together by the Chair and Vice Chair within sixty days after all nine politically appointed members are selected, subject to approval by a majority vote of those members. The Chair and Vice Chair can together hire additional staff as needed for the Commission to do its work. The Director and staff can be hired without following the normal competitive service rules and can be paid without following the normal classification and pay scale rules, except that no one can be paid more than the equivalent of a level five position on the Executive Schedule. Anyone hired will be treated as a federal employee for purposes of leave, injury compensation, retirement, health insurance, and other benefit programs. Any federal government employee can work for the Commission temporarily without the Commission paying their salary, and they keep their regular employment rights, status, and privileges without interruption. The Commission can hire experts and consultants following normal government rules but at rates not exceeding the daily rate for a level four Executive Schedule position. Each Commission member can be paid up to the daily equivalent of the annual basic pay for a level four Executive Schedule position for each day they actually work for the Commission. While away from home or their regular workplace doing Commission work, members can get travel expenses including daily allowances for meals and lodging the same way as people who work for the government part time. The Federal Advisory Committee Act does not apply to the Commission.

sections.title: Section 7  Termination
sections.text: The Commission will end ninety days after it submits its report to Congress.

sections.title: Section 8  Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: To carry out this law, Congress is authorized to spend twenty million dollars.
